_note: Fixed-overhead probe. A near-trivial question that needs no history. Any baseline-vs-entire cost delta here is pure fixed Entire overhead (skill-file load + `entire status`), not retrieval value. Subtract this delta from the other tasks to estimate marginal retrieval savings. Compare against the per-trial `entire_marginal~` figure in the summary.
prompt: In one or two sentences, state this project's name and which package manager / build tool it uses. Answer directly from the current repository.
acceptanceCriteria: Names the package manager or build tool correctly.
Stays brief and does not investigate project history.

_note: TEMPLATE — fill in a real removed/changed behavior before running with --include-drafts. This is the scenario where Entire should win on cost: the answer is in history, not current source, so baseline must crawl full diff history while Entire reads one checkpoint. Replace <BEHAVIOR> and the file hints with a feature that was actually removed or significantly reworked in this repo's history.
prompt: Explain why <BEHAVIOR> was removed (or significantly changed) and what replaced it. The current source no longer contains it, so ground the answer in recorded history — commits, diffs, and Entire checkpoint/session evidence — not in the present code.
acceptanceCriteria: Correctly identifies when and why <BEHAVIOR> was removed or changed.
Cites the specific commit(s) and/or checkpoint/session evidence that records the decision.
Describes what replaced it, if anything.
Does not fabricate a rationale that the recorded history does not support.

_note: TEMPLATE — set `base` to a commit AFTER <BEHAVIOR> was removed, and fill <BEHAVIOR>. Baseline must reconstruct the removed implementation from scratch by reading diffs; the Entire arm can recover the prior implementation/intent from the checkpoint that captured it. This is a code task, so checks gate acceptance.
prompt: Restore <BEHAVIOR> that used to exist in this project but was later removed. Match the original behavior and integration points as closely as the recorded history supports. Keep the change narrow.
acceptanceCriteria: Re-implements <BEHAVIOR> consistent with how it previously worked.
Wires it back into the same integration points it used before.
Keeps the change narrow and avoids unrelated edits.

_note: TEMPLATE for the cross-session continuation scenario — Entire's home-field workflow. The harness runs `seed.prompt` first (session A, Entire-enabled) to make PARTIAL progress and checkpoint it, then both arms continue from that seed commit. Baseline has only the half-finished code and must reconstruct intent/approach; the Entire arm can read the prior session's checkpoint. Seed once via `node scripts/token-benchmark.mjs seed --task continue-partial-feature --execute`, then run with `--task continue-partial-feature --seed-base <sha> --include-drafts`. Replace <FEATURE> with something real for this repo.

seed.prompt: Begin implementing <FEATURE>. Make partial progress only: lay out the file structure, stub the main functions with clear TODOs, and write a short plan in a comment or PLAN file. Do NOT finish the implementation. Explain your intended approach in your final message.
acceptanceCriteria: Creates the skeleton and a written plan for <FEATURE> without completing it.

prompt: A previous session started implementing <FEATURE> but did not finish. Continue and complete it, following the approach the earlier work established. Keep the change narrow and consistent with the existing partial work.
acceptanceCriteria: Completes <FEATURE> consistent with the partial work's established approach.
Does not contradict or rip out the seed session's design without reason.
